title: Knot types of periodic Reeb orbits and their role in 4-dimensional symplectic topology
speaker: Umberto Hryniewicz
abstract:
Remote Access
This talk, which is based on two joint works, one with Pedro Salomão and Richard Siefring and another with Michael Hutchings and Vinicius Ramos, revolves around the role that restrictions on the knot types of periodic Reeb orbits imposed by the assumption of dynamical convexity plays in 4-dimensional symplectic topology. For instance, for dynamically convex star-shaped domains in a 4-dimensional symplectic vector space, the minimal action among periodic Reeb orbits in the boundary which are unknotted and have self-linking number -1, called Hopf orbits, satisfies the axioms of a normalized symplectic capacity. This shows that this number is not larger than the cylindrical capacity. A result of Edtmair establishes the other inequality, and these two results combined yield that the minimal action of a Hopf orbit is equal to the cylindrical capacity of such a domain. We will discuss why is this equal to the first ECH capacity, which then explains the latter capacity in simple and purely symplectic geometric terms, with no need of Seiberg-Witten theory. We will also discuss why several transverse knot types in the standard contact 3-sphere cannot be realized as periodic Reeb orbits of a dynamically convex contact form.

proof_stages.label: Knot-filtered Reeb dynamics as the guiding problem
proof_stages.summary: The talk formulates two overarching questions: whether symplectic invariants can be read from actions of Reeb orbits of prescribed knot/link type, and whether transverse knot/link types measure the topological complexity of a three-dimensional flow.

proof_stages.label: Hopf fibers and Hopf links as the minimal periodic-orbit skeleton
proof_stages.summary: On standard contact S^3, Hopf fibers are introduced as unknots with self-linking -1, known to occur in every Reeb flow, and the conjectural next minimal structure is a Hopf link, against which simple, chaotic, and maximally knot-rich flows are compared.

proof_stages.label: Dynamical convexity and knot-type restrictions
proof_stages.summary: The speaker introduces dynamical convexity for star-shaped domains in R^4 and states that a symplectically slice closed characteristic on a dynamically convex boundary must be a Hopf fiber, with finite collections of Hopf-fiber orbits forming fibered links/global sections.

proof_stages.label: Quasipositive and slice-genus sources of forbidden knots
proof_stages.summary: Quasipositive links are related to transverse C-links and symplectic slicing surfaces, so slice-genus-zero quasipositive knots give many concrete knot types excluded by the dynamical-convexity theorem.

proof_stages.label: Fast holomorphic planes from a symplectic slicing disk
proof_stages.summary: The proof sketch converts the symplectic slicing disk into a fast finite-energy holomorphic plane, uses Conley-Zehnder index at least three and exponential weights to obtain a two-dimensional moduli space, pushes a marked point toward the boundary, rules out SFT breaking by dynamical convexity, and uses intersection theory to conclude the asymptotic orbit is an embedded unknot/Hopf fiber.

proof_stages.label: Definition and capacity property of the Hopf action invariant
proof_stages.summary: For a symplectic four-manifold, C_Hopf is defined as the supremum over dynamically convex star-shaped subdomains of the minimal action among Hopf-fiber Reeb orbits, and it is stated to be a normalized symplectic capacity agreeing with the first ECH capacity on dynamically convex star-shaped domains.

proof_stages.label: Comparison with classical capacities and open extensions
proof_stages.summary: The Hopf capacity is compared with the cylindrical capacity and known convex-domain results, Hofer-Wysocki-Zehnder’s minimum-action global-section question is reformulated as C_Hopf equaling the minimum action, and possible extensions via second Hopf-link orbits, higher ECH capacities, and finite-energy foliations are proposed.

examples.name: Anosov-Katok Reeb flows with only two periodic orbits
examples.note: These constructions show that having only the Hopf-link periodic orbits does not by itself make the flow dynamically simple, since the return dynamics can still have positive entropy.
examples.engagement_reason: The Anosov-Katok flows are cited as famous examples with only two periodic orbits and possible positive entropy, but no construction is carried out.

passes.reason: Anosov-Katok Reeb flows have the minimal two periodic orbits yet are used to refute the inference that only a Hopf link means dynamically simple.
